title: tempssm: State-space modeling for temperature time series in R
text: R package tempssm provides tools state-space analysis temperature time series, focusing linear Gaussian state-space models estimated via Kalman filtering smoothing implemented KFAS package (Helske, 2017).

title: tempssm: State-space modeling for temperature time series in R
text: Designed temperature time series arbitrary seasonal frequencies; currently validated primarily monthly data Estimates latent states using linear Gaussian state-space models combined Kalman filtering smoothing Models temperature dynamics sum interpretable latent components, including long-term trend, seasonal cycle, autoregressive structure, optional exogenous effects Allows users specify arbitrary order autoregressive component (default: AR(1)) Implements time-series cross-validation model evaluation

title: tempssm: State-space modeling for temperature time series in R
text: tempssm provides domain-focused workflow analyzing temperature time series linear Gaussian state-space models. brings together model construction, component extraction, uncertainty summaries, residual diagnostics, visualization, time-series cross-validation single R package interface tailored temperature applications. package builds established statistical methodology, including linear Gaussian state-space modeling, Kalman filtering, Kalman smoothing. Model estimation handled KFAS package, provides general framework state-space models R. initial implementation adapted supplementary code provided Baba (2024), accompanying Baba et al. (2024), analyzed sea temperature trends using linear Gaussian state-space model. supplementary code publicly available : https://github.com/logics--blue/sea-temperature-trend-jogashima Compared prior implementation, tempssm extends workflow reusable R package interface input validation, documented S3 methods, tests, diagnostics, cross-validation utilities, examples broader temperature time-series analysis. next section describes state-space model used tempssm clarifies trend, seasonal, autoregressive, exogenous components represented.

title: tempssm: State-space modeling for temperature time series in R
text: consider extended version Basic Structural Time Series Model (BSTSM) describe temperature time series explicit long-term trend, seasonal variability, auto-regressive component. observation equation given yt=αt+vt. y_t = \alpha_t + v_t. \tag{1}  tt denotes time index, yty_t observed temperature, αt\alpha_t latent state, vtv_t observation error. latent state decomposed αt=μt+st+rt+Et. \alpha_t = \mu_t + s_t + r_t + E_t. \tag{2}  μt\mu_t level (trend), sts_t seasonal component, rtr_t stationary autoregressive component, EtE_t contribution exogenous variables. level component follows second-order stochastic process: μt=2μt−1−μt−2+ζt,ζt∼𝒩(0,σζ2). \mu_t = 2\mu_{t-1} - \mu_{t-2} + \zeta_t, \qquad \zeta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\zeta^2). \tag{3}  ζt\zeta_t process error level component. seasonal component modeled sum--zero constraint: st=−∑=t−ft−1si+ωt,ωt∼𝒩(0,σω2). s_t = - \sum_{=t-f}^{t-1} s_i + \omega_t, \qquad \omega_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\omega^2). \tag{4}  ωt\omega_t process error seasonal component ff denotes seasonal frequency (e.g., f=12f=12 monthly data). formulation ensures seasonal effects identifiable comparable across different temporal resolutions. enforcing sum--zero constraint one full seasonal cycle, seasonal component captures recurring deviations underlying trend without introducing long-term drift. models without seasonal component, seasonal term sts_t set zero omitted satate equation. autoregressive component follows llth-order autoregressive (AR) process: rt=ϕ1rt−1+ϕ2rt−2+⋯+ϕlrt−l+τt,τt∼𝒩(0,στ2). r_t = \phi_1 r_{t-1} + \phi_2 r_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_l r_{t-l} + \tau_t, \qquad \tau_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\tau^2). \tag{5}  , τt\tau_t denotes process error autoregressive component. process error terms (ζt\zeta_t, ωt\omega_t, τt\tau_t) assumed mutually independent normally distributed. exogenous component defined Et=β1x1,t+β2x2,t+⋯+βmxm,t. E_t = \beta_1 x_{1,t} + \beta_2 x_{2,t} + \cdots + \beta_m x_{m,t}. \tag{6}  exogenous term EtE_t represents influence external factors, may include large-scale climate indices, regional environmental variables, physically motivated predictors relevant observed temperature time series. exogenous variable enters model linearly time-invariant regression coefficient (β1,β2,…,βm\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_m), allowing magnitude direction contribution estimated jointly latent state components. applying model observational data, total number parameters estimated (kk) depends order autoregressive component number exogenous variables included. example, model second-order autoregressive component exogenous covariates, six parameters estimated: observation error variance, process error variances associated level, seasonal, autoregressive components, first- second-order autoregressive coefficients (ϕ1\phi_1 ϕ2\phi_2). general case ll-order autoregressive component mm exogenous variables, total number parameters k=4+l+mk = 4 + l + m. core implementation parameter estimation procedure based supplementary code provided Baba et al. (2024). Parameter estimation performed using two-step optimization strategy recommended Helske (2017). first step, model parameters estimated using Nelder-Mead method (Nelder & Mead, 1965) user-specified initial values. resulting estimates used initial values second optimization step based BFGS algorithm (Shanno, 1970). main model-fitting function, tempssm(), returns filtering smoothing estimates. Unless otherwise stated, results presented vignette based smoothed estimates.

what: Applying a Linear Gaussian State-Space Model
title: tempssm: State-space modeling for temperature time series in R
text: , fit linear Gaussian state-space model univariate SST time series using AR(1) process state dynamics. Preliminary comparisons among AR(1)–AR(3) models identified AR(1) preferred model. keep vignette concise, AR(1) analysis presented. Users interested evaluating alternative autoregressive structures can readily extend workflow higher AR orders. model summary provides parameter estimates diagnostic information assessing model adequacy.
code: # model with first-order autoregressive component res <- tempssm(niigata_sst) # AR(1), the default model summary(res) ## tempssm summary ## ----------------- ## Call: ## tempssm(temp_data = niigata_sst) ##  ## Model fit: ##   Likelihood type: diffuse  ##   Log-likelihood : -277.95  ##   k              : 5  ##   AIC            : 565.9  ##   Converged      : TRUE  ##  ## Variance parameters: ##   Observation (H): 0.005985633  ##   State (Q trend): 1.268116e-07  ##   State (Q season): 0.001346144  ##   State (Q ar): 0.4097882  ##  ## Components of auto-regression: ##   Order of AR: 1  ##   Coefficient of AR1: 0.7443 AIC(res) ## [1] 565.8955

title: Linear Gaussian State-Space Models for Temperature Time Series
text: Fits linear Gaussian state-space models temperature time series. Represents temperature dynamics using interpretable latent components: level, seasonal variation, autoregressive structure, optional exogenous effects. Supports arbitrary integer seasonal frequencies, current examples validation focus primarily monthly temperature data. Allows autoregressive order specified user. Provides S3 methods summaries, diagnostics, accessors, plots. Includes time-series cross-validation tools model evaluation.

Model estimation handled KFAS package, provides general framework state-space models R. initial implementation adapted supplementary code provided Baba (2024), accompanying Baba et al. (2024), analyzed sea temperature trends using linear Gaussian state-space model. supplementary code publicly available : https://github.com/logics--blue/sea-temperature-trend-jogashima Compared prior implementation, tempssm extends workflow reusable R package interface input validation, documented S3 methods, tests, diagnostics, cross-validation utilities, examples broader temperature time-series analysis.

what: Load the Package and Example Data
title: Linear Gaussian State-Space Models for Temperature Time Series
text: example uses monthly sea surface temperature (SST) data Niigata, Japan, February 2002 December 2023.
code: library(tempssm) data(niigata_sst)

title: Linear Gaussian State-Space Models for Temperature Time Series
text: function tempssm() fits linear Gaussian state-space model temperature time series. returned object S3 object class "tempssm". Results can inspected standard methods. panels show estimated model components; gray ribbons indicate pointwise 95% confidence intervals. example, estimated level component suggests gradual increase SST, average annual increase approximately 0.05 °C. ggplot2-style interface also available: default, plot(res) autoplot(res) produce component plot.
code: res <- tempssm(niigata_sst) summary(res) plot(res) autoplot(res)

title: Linear Gaussian State-Space Models for Temperature Time Series
text: already ts object, pass directly tempssm(). monthly temperature data stored CSV file, prepare columns named Year, Month, Temp. Use NA missing temperature values, keep corresponding Year Month entries. CSV file comma-separated UTF-8 encoded. read_monthly_temp_ts() uses base R’s ts() representation internally. can also construct ts object manually finer control needed.
code: res <- tempssm(my_ts) Year,Month,Temp 2010,8,13.6 2010,9,6.8 2010,10,NA 2010,11,-1.4 ... my_ts <- tempssm::read_monthly_temp_ts("temperature.csv") res <- tempssm(my_ts)

title: Convert a data frame of monthly temperature time series to a ts object — convert_monthly_df_to_ts
text: function converts data frame containing monthly temperature time series R ts object frequency 12. input data frame must contain following columns: Date date column indicating time index. exact day month uniquely defined, arbitrary day (e.g., first day month) may used. Temp Monthly temperature values. Missing values represented NA. function assumes input data represent regularly spaced monthly time series. missing months detected Date column, warning issued explicit NA values inserted. expected format : output regular monthly ts object. Months represented equally spaced observations frequency = 12; month lengths converted fixed number days.

code: ## Create a data frame of monthly temperature data df <- data.frame(   Date = as.Date(c(     "2001-01-01",     "2001-02-01",     "2001-03-01",     "2001-04-01",     "2001-05-01"   )),   Temp = c(10.4, 8.2, NA, 13.6, 16.1) )  ## Convert to a monthly ts object temp_ts <- convert_monthly_df_to_ts(df) #> Converted data frame to monthly time series with 5 observations  ## Inspect the result temp_ts #>       Jan  Feb  Mar  Apr  May #> 2001 10.4  8.2   NA 13.6 16.1
